stem: The nurse assesses the client who is experiencing increasing respiratory distress. Based on the trends in the client's clinical data, which action is the highest priority for the nurse to take at this time?

options.text: Administer a 500 mL bolus of 0.9% sodium chloride.
Elevate the head of the bed to 90 degrees.
Encourage the client to use the incentive spirometer.

itemContext.sbar:
S: This is the nurse for Jameson Reyes, a 62-year-old male in room 402, admitted for pneumonia. He is now in acute respiratory distress.
B: Mr. Reyes has a history of COPD and hypertension and was diagnosed with influenza A last week. He is allergic to Penicillin. He lives alone in a rural area, which may pose challenges for post-discharge follow-up.
A: At 06:00, the client is anxious, restless, and confused. His respiratory rate is 32/min and labored with accessory muscle use. SpO₂ is 88% on a 6L nasal cannula. Vitals are trending poorly: HR 110, BP 160/90. Auscultation reveals coarse crackles bilaterally. An ABG at 05:45 showed acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 60 mmHg, PaO₂ 55 mmHg).
R: Immediate intervention is required. I recommend initiating BiPAP to improve ventilation and will be notifying the rapid response team. We should prepare for potential intubation if he fails to improve.

tabs.content:

05:30155/88 mmHg (110)105 bpm30/min89% on 5L NC101.3°F (38.5°C)
06:00160/90 mmHg (113)110 bpm32/min88% on 6L NC101.3°F (38.5°C)

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
General: Anxious, restless, in moderate respiratory distress.
Respiratory: Tachypneic with a rate of 32/min. Labored breathing with use of accessory muscles. Coarse crackles auscultated throughout all lung fields bilaterally.
Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, S1/S2 audible, no murmurs. Pulses 2+ peripherally.
Neurological: Alert but increasingly confused. Oriented to person only.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content:
Chest X-ray (Admission, 3 days ago)
FINDINGS: Bilateral interstitial and alveolar infiltrates, more pronounced in the lower lobes, consistent with multifocal pneumonia. No evidence of large pleural effusion or pneumothorax. Cardiomediastinal silhouette is within normal limits.
IMPRESSION: Findings are consistent with bilateral pneumonia.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content:
Order Date/TimeActionFrequency/Status
Day 1, 14:00Admit to medical unit with telemetry monitoringStanding
Day 1, 14:00Oxygen therapy to maintain SpO₂ > 92%Standing, Titrate
Day 1, 14:00IV fluids: 0.9% Sodium Chloride75 mL/hr
Day 1, 14:00Regular diet, as toleratedStanding
Day 1, 14:00Activity: Bed rest with bathroom privilegesStanding

rationale.correct: The client is in acute hypercapnic respiratory failure, evidenced by the low pH (7.28) and high PaCO₂ (60 mmHg). Non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (BiPAP) is the highest priority because it provides immediate ventilatory support to decrease the work of breathing, improve alveolar ventilation to expel CO₂, and enhance oxygenation, thereby directly treating the life-threatening condition.
rationale.incorrect: Administering an IV fluid bolus is contraindicated as it can worsen pulmonary edema in a client with pneumonia or ARDS. Elevating the head of the bed is a helpful supportive measure but does not correct the underlying ventilation failure. Using an incentive spirometer is inappropriate for a client in acute distress as it would further increase the work of breathing and cause fatigue.

answerBreakdown.content: Correct. BiPAP is the priority intervention to address both hypoxemia and hypercapnia by providing ventilatory support, reducing the work of breathing, and improving gas exchange.
Incorrect. This client's respiratory failure is not due to hypovolemia. An IV fluid bolus could increase hydrostatic pressure and worsen pulmonary edema, further impairing gas exchange.

Incorrect. While elevating the head of the bed is a beneficial supportive measure to improve lung expansion, it is not the priority therapeutic intervention required to reverse acute respiratory failure.

Incorrect. Incentive spirometry is used for lung expansion post-operatively or to prevent atelectasis. It is contraindicated in a client with acute respiratory distress as it increases respiratory muscle fatigue.

clinicalPearls: Acute hypercapnic respiratory failure is defined by a PaCO₂ > 45 mmHg and a pH < 7.35, indicating inadequate ventilation.
Non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV) like BiPAP is a first-line treatment for COPD exacerbations and acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema to avoid intubation.
A key sign of impending respiratory arrest is a change in mental status (e.g., confusion, lethargy), which is caused by hypercapnia and/or severe hypoxemia.
rationale.questionTrap: The trap is choosing a correct but lower-priority 'supportive' measure (like elevating the head of the bed) over a direct therapeutic intervention (BiPAP) that addresses the life-threatening pathophysiology.
rationale.mnemonic: For signs of respiratory distress, remember 'WOB-UP': W - Work of breathing increased, O - Oxygen saturation decreased, B - Breathing rate abnormal (tachy/bradypnea), U - Use of accessory muscles, P - Positioning (tripod).
